def: A distressing inner restlessness with a compulsion to move or pace — a common early antipsychotic side effect.
Sudden sustained muscle spasm (neck, eyes, tongue) soon after a dopamine blocker; treated with IM/IV anticholinergics.
An acute dystonic reaction forcing sustained upward or lateral deviation of the eyes.
Severe backward arching of the spine from sustained muscle spasm; a form of acute dystonia.
Antipsychotic-induced tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia mimicking Parkinson disease, from D2 blockade.
Late-onset, often irreversible repetitive movements (esp. face and tongue) after prolonged dopamine-blocker exposure.
Rhythmic involuntary shaking; a fine tremor is common with lithium/SSRIs, a coarse tremor suggests lithium toxicity.
A drop in blood pressure on standing, causing dizziness or fainting; common with alpha-1 blockade.
High prolactin from D2 blockade, causing galactorrhea, amenorrhea, sexual dysfunction, or gynecomastia.
Inappropriate milk secretion unrelated to nursing — a sign of elevated prolactin.
Absence of menstrual periods; can result from antipsychotic-induced high prolactin.
Excess saliva and drooling — a characteristic, paradoxical clozapine effect.
A prolonged, painful erection unrelated to arousal; a urologic emergency and a rare trazodone side effect.
A distorted or unpleasant sense of taste — e.g., the metallic taste with zopiclone.
Numbness of the tongue and mouth — an expected, temporary effect of sublingual asenapine.
Profuse sweating; a feature of serotonin syndrome, NMS, and autonomic crises.
Exaggerated deep tendon reflexes; with clonus, a key sign of serotonin syndrome.
Rhythmic involuntary muscle contractions triggered by stretch; a hallmark of serotonin toxicity.
Loss of coordinated movement (unsteady gait, clumsiness); a sign of lithium toxicity.
Dangerously elevated body temperature; a feature of serotonin syndrome, NMS, and severe TCA overdose.
Loss of the ability to feel pleasure or interest — a core depressive and negative symptom.
Visible slowing of movement, speech, and thought, seen in severe depression.
A sense of detachment from oneself or surroundings (feeling dreamlike); a hallmark ketamine/esketamine effect.
Impaired libido, arousal, or orgasm; a common, often under-reported SSRI/SNRI side effect.
Low sex-hormone production causing reduced libido and sexual dysfunction; can follow chronic opioid use.
Loss of memory formation; benzodiazepines like midazolam cause anterograde amnesia during procedures.
An abnormally slow heart rate; watch for it with cholinesterase inhibitors and alpha-2 agonists.
A sharp blood-pressure rise after abruptly stopping an alpha-2 agonist like clonidine — so never stop it cold.
Inability to fully empty the bladder — a common anticholinergic side effect, worse with prostate enlargement.
Nerve damage causing tingling, numbness, or pain in the limbs; can follow B6 depletion with hydrazine MAOIs.
Sudden loss of muscle tone triggered by emotion, classically part of narcolepsy.
Brief inability to move when falling asleep or waking; can occur with orexin antagonists.
Slowed height and weight gain in children on long-term stimulants; monitor on a growth chart.
The 'added' symptoms of psychosis — delusions, hallucinations, and disorganized speech or behaviour.
The 'deficit' symptoms of schizophrenia — blunted affect, apathy, social withdrawal, and poverty of speech.
A fixed false belief held despite clear contrary evidence, often persecutory.
A perception without an external stimulus — e.g., hearing voices or seeing things that aren't there.
A neurodevelopmental disorder of inattention and/or hyperactivity-impulsivity; stimulants are first-line.
A chronic disorder of excessive daytime sleepiness, sometimes with cataplexy, linked to loss of orexin signaling.
A mood disorder with ≥2 weeks of low mood or loss of interest plus changes in sleep, appetite, energy, and concentration.
An abnormally elevated or irritable mood with high energy, reduced sleep, and impaired judgment — the manic pole of bipolar.
A milder, shorter form of mania without psychosis or marked functional impairment.
A disorder combining features of psychosis with mood episodes (mania or depression).
Loss of contact with reality — hallucinations, delusions, or disorganized thought.
Visual hallucinations and delusions in Parkinson disease; treated without worsening motor function (e.g., pimavanserin).
An acute, fluctuating disturbance of attention and awareness from an underlying medical cause — treat the cause.
Progressive decline in memory and other cognitive domains that erodes daily function.
Excessive motor and verbal activity with heightened arousal, sometimes escalating to aggression.
A syndrome of abnormal movement and reactivity — immobility, mutism, rigidity, or excitement; severe cases may need ECT.
Chronic, excessive, free-floating worry present most days for >6 months, with tension, fatigue, and restlessness.
An abrupt surge of intense fear peaking within minutes: palpitations, sweating, trembling, and breathlessness.
Intense fear of scrutiny or negative evaluation in social or performance settings, driving avoidance.
Intrusive, unwanted obsessions and repetitive compulsions performed to reduce the resulting distress.
A trauma-related disorder with re-experiencing, avoidance, hyperarousal, and negative shifts in mood and cognition.
Depression during pregnancy or after childbirth; treated with the best-studied agents at the lowest effective dose.
A neurodevelopmental disorder of multiple motor and vocal tics beginning in childhood.
A prolonged seizure, or repeated seizures without recovery between them — a neurologic emergency.
Pain arising from nerve damage or dysfunction; treated with agents like duloxetine, TCAs, and gabapentinoids.
Nerve damage from diabetes causing burning, tingling limb pain — a duloxetine indication.
Sudden, severe electric-shock facial pain in the trigeminal nerve distribution; carbamazepine is first-line.
Recurrent binge eating without compensatory purging; lisdexamfetamine is FDA-approved for it.
An urge to move the legs with uncomfortable sensations, worse at rest and at night; can drive insomnia.
Dissatisfaction with sleep quantity or quality despite adequate opportunity, with daytime impairment; CBT-I is first-line.
A pattern of impaired control over alcohol use; anti-craving options include naltrexone and acamprosate.
Autonomic and neurologic hyperactivity after stopping heavy drinking; can progress to seizures or delirium tremens.
The most severe alcohol withdrawal (~48–96 h): confusion, agitation, and autonomic instability; potentially fatal.
Compulsive opioid use despite harm; first-line treatment is opioid agonist therapy (buprenorphine/naloxone or methadone).
Sweating, cramps, diarrhea, and dysphoria after stopping opioids; objective signs are scored on scales like COWS.
Treating opioid use disorder with methadone or buprenorphine to suppress withdrawal and blunt cravings.
Abrupt, severe withdrawal triggered when a high-affinity partial agonist/antagonist displaces a full opioid — e.g., early buprenorphine.
Nicotine dependence; first-line cessation pharmacotherapy is varenicline, nicotine replacement, or bupropion.
A potentially life-threatening excess of serotonergic activity: agitation, clonus, hyperreflexia, fever, and autonomic instability.
A rare, life-threatening dopamine-blocker reaction: fever, lead-pipe rigidity, autonomic instability, and very high CK.
Movement disturbances from D2 blockade: acute dystonia, akathisia, parkinsonism, and (late) tardive dyskinesia.
A lengthened cardiac QT interval on ECG that raises the risk of torsades de pointes.
A polymorphic ventricular tachycardia triggered by QT prolongation; can be fatal.
Low serum sodium; SSRIs and oxcarbazepine/carbamazepine can cause it, risking headache, confusion, and seizures.
Excess ADH causing euvolemic hyponatremia with concentrated urine; SSRIs are a common cause in older women.
Kidney failure to concentrate urine (polyuria, thirst); lithium causes it and it does not respond to desmopressin.
A dangerous loss of neutrophils raising serious infection risk; clozapine's key hazard, requiring ANC monitoring.
A low neutrophil count; severe neutropenia (ANC <0.5) means stopping clozapine and generally not rechallenging.
A severe, blistering skin and mucosal drug reaction; a risk with lamotrigine and carbamazepine.
Clustered weight gain, insulin resistance, dyslipidemia, and hypertension; olanzapine and clozapine are the worst offenders.
Flu-like symptoms, insomnia, nausea, imbalance, and 'brain zaps' after stopping an antidepressant; worse with short half-lives.
A dangerous BP surge when MAOI patients eat aged tyramine-rich foods: pounding occipital headache, hypertension, palpitations.
Antimuscarinic overload — 'mad, red, hot, dry, and blind': delirium, flushing, fever, dry skin, and blurred vision.
Neuro-GI toxicity above ~1.5 mmol/L: coarse tremor, ataxia, confusion, vomiting; precipitated by dehydration, NSAIDs, diuretics.
Drug-induced liver damage; a concern with atomoxetine, nefazodone, and hydrazine MAOIs.
Dangerously slowed, shallow breathing; risk rises when opioids or benzodiazepines combine with other CNS depressants.
Excess acid in the blood; topiramate's carbonic-anhydrase inhibition can cause a non-anion-gap form.
A sudden rise in eye pressure (painful red eye, blurred vision) — an emergency; topiramate and anticholinergics can trigger it.
Inflammation of the heart muscle; an early clozapine risk (chest pain, dyspnea, rising troponin/CRP) in the first weeks.
Severe gut slowing that can halt bowel movement; a clozapine risk worsened by anticholinergics.
Muscle breakdown releasing myoglobin and CK, risking acute kidney injury; a complication of NMS.
The time for a drug's blood level to fall by half; it sets dosing frequency and how quickly a drug clears.
Gradually adjusting a dose up or down to reach the target effect while limiting side effects.
An inactive drug the body converts into its active form — e.g., lisdexamfetamine into dextroamphetamine.
A pharmacologically active breakdown product of a drug that can prolong or add to its effect.
The safety margin between an effective dose and a toxic one; narrow-index drugs like lithium need level monitoring.
Measuring blood drug levels to keep them in a target range — used for lithium, valproate, clozapine, and some TCAs.
A drug-free interval when switching medications to avoid dangerous interactions — e.g., before starting an MAOI.
A drug that binds a receptor but only partly activates it, capping the effect (e.g., aripiprazole at D2, buprenorphine at mu).
A large initial dose given to reach a therapeutic level quickly — e.g., valproate in acute mania.
A liver conjugation pathway (not CYP) that clears drugs like lorazepam — sparing patients with liver disease.
When a drug speeds up its own metabolism over weeks (classically carbamazepine), lowering its level and needing re-titration.
A major liver enzyme; strong inhibitors (azoles, macrolides, grapefruit) raise its substrates' levels, inducers lower them.
A liver enzyme with genetic 'poor metabolizers'; inhibitors like fluoxetine, paroxetine, and bupropion raise substrate levels.
A liver enzyme induced by smoking and potently inhibited by fluvoxamine; it clears clozapine, caffeine, and theophylline.
A liver enzyme with poor-metabolizer variants; relevant to citalopram/escitalopram dose caps and clobazam clearance.
One mirror-image form of a molecule; a drug may be a single enantiomer (escitalopram) or a racemic mix of both.
Swapping hydrogen for heavier deuterium to slow metabolism and smooth drug levels — as in deutetrabenazine.
A drug that binds a receptor and produces the opposite effect to an agonist — e.g., pimavanserin at 5-HT2A.
A drug that boosts a receptor's response to its natural ligand rather than activating it directly — e.g., benzodiazepines at GABA-A.
A drug mimicking adrenaline (e.g., pseudoephedrine); dangerous with MAOIs, which can trigger a hypertensive crisis.
An agent capable of causing fetal malformations; valproate is the most teratogenic mood stabilizer.
A person whose genetics make a CYP enzyme sluggish, so levels of that enzyme's drug substrates run high.
When tolerance to one drug carries over to another in the same class, letting one substitute for another (e.g., in withdrawal).
A long-acting injectable antipsychotic given every few weeks to months to improve adherence.
A regulator's strongest safety warning on a drug label, flagging serious or life-threatening risks.
Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor — first-line antidepressants and anxiolytics (e.g., sertraline, escitalopram).
Serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (e.g., venlafaxine, duloxetine); adds noradrenergic action and can raise BP.
Monoamine oxidase inhibitor — potent antidepressants requiring a low-tyramine diet and drug washout periods.
Reversible inhibitor of MAO-A (moclobemide); tyramine can displace it, so dietary rules are far looser than with classic MAOIs.
Older antidepressant class (e.g., amitriptyline); effective but anticholinergic and lethal in overdose (wide QRS, arrhythmia).
GABA-A enhancers used for anxiety, seizures, and sedation; carry tolerance, dependence, and withdrawal risk.
Non-benzodiazepine hypnotics (zolpidem, zopiclone) acting at the GABA-A benzodiazepine site, marketed for insomnia.
Dual orexin receptor antagonist (e.g., lemborexant, daridorexant) — non-addictive hypnotics that block wake-promoting orexin.
A drug that depletes presynaptic dopamine (valbenazine, tetrabenazine, deutetrabenazine) — used to treat tardive dyskinesia.
Serotonin antagonist/reuptake inhibitor (trazodone, nefazodone) — sedating antidepressants.
First-generation ('typical') antipsychotic (e.g., haloperidol); potent D2 blockers with more EPS.
Second-generation ('atypical') antipsychotic (e.g., risperidone, olanzapine); fewer EPS but more metabolic risk.
Boosts acetylcholine to modestly ease dementia symptoms (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine); can cause bradycardia and GI upset.
Blocks adrenergic beta receptors; propranolol treats akathisia and performance-anxiety tremor and palpitations.
Reduces sympathetic outflow for calming and BP-lowering effects (clonidine, guanfacine); causes sedation and needs tapering.
Blocks alpha-1 receptors; prazosin is used for PTSD nightmares but causes first-dose orthostatic hypotension.
Older GABA-A sedatives (e.g., phenobarbital) with a narrow safety margin, high overdose lethality, and dangerous withdrawal.
GABA-A-modulating steroids (brexanolone, zuranolone) used as rapid-acting treatments for postpartum depression.
Blocks the NMDA glutamate receptor; memantine uses this in dementia, and ketamine/esketamine for depression.
Blocks acetylcholine's muscarinic effects; treats acute dystonia (benztropine) but causes dry mouth, constipation, confusion.
Inducing a brief therapeutic seizure under anesthesia; used for severe, psychotic, or refractory depression and catatonia.
Cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia — the durable first-line treatment (stimulus control, sleep restriction).
A list of medications (e.g., benzodiazepines, anticholinergics) best avoided in older adults due to harm risk.
The Clinical Opiate Withdrawal Scale — objective withdrawal signs used to time safe buprenorphine induction.
The Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale — rates OCD severity and tracks treatment response.
A 9-item self-report questionnaire measuring depression severity and tracking response to treatment.
Brief bedside cognitive screening tests (MoCA, MMSE) used to detect and track dementia.
A muscle enzyme released with muscle damage; markedly elevated in NMS and rhabdomyolysis.
A measure of kidney filtration; low values raise levels of renally cleared drugs like lithium and paliperidone.
